ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a coconut juice entry; the flavoured/sweetened variant is not separately coded. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers coconut water/juice drinks broadly; no specific flavoured variant code exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for flavoured coconut juice drink. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is approximately 250 ml (one standard carton or cup), equivalent to 250 g, providing about 85 kcal and 19.3 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured Coconut Juice Drink — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Flavoured Coconut Juice Drink (cr_id D03070004). All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Coconut water and lightly sweetened juice drinks — analogous GI estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for flavoured coconut juice drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 54 (low, ≤54) based on the closest analogous category: plain coconut water and lightly sweetened dilute juice drinks with similar sugar content (~7–8 g/100 ml) and low fibre, which cluster in the GI 45–54 range per University of Sydney GI Database entries. A GI of 54 is the upper bound of the low category and is the most defensible conservative estimate for this product. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (54 × 19.3 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 10.4 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.7 g sugar per 100 ml; low sodium (1 mg/100 ml) but added sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this flavoured coconut juice drink due to its sugar-sweetened beverage status (7.7 g sugar/100 ml), which independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance, and should choose plain unsweetened coconut water for natural potassium benefit without added sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.7 g sugar per 100 ml and only 0.2 g dietary fibre; rapid glycaemic response from liquid sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this flavoured coconut juice drink as it is a sugar-sweetened beverage (7.7 g sugar/100 ml, estimated GI ~54, GL ~10.4 per 250 ml serving) with negligible fibre, triggering the ADA SSB avoidance recommendation and producing a rapid postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk; fat content is negligible (0.02 g/100 ml)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit this flavoured coconut juice drink because habitual sugar-sweetened beverage intake (7.7 g sugar/100 ml) is independently associated with elevated triglycerides, weight gain, and CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines, despite its very low fat and sodium content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (7.7 g/100 ml) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no meaningful anti-inflammatory components present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this flavoured coconut juice drink as its added sugar content (7.7 g/100 ml) promotes inflammatory mediators, and the absence of omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or meaningful fibre provides no anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured Coconut Juice Drink is a processed, sweetened beverage made from coconut juice concentrate diluted with water and enhanced with added flavours and sugar. It is commercially produced and widely available in Singapore and Southeast Asia, typically sold in 250 ml cartons or cups. The drink has a light, mildly sweet coconut flavour with a thin, watery texture — noticeably sweeter and less natural-tasting than plain unsweetened coconut water. Per 100 ml, it provides 34 kcal, 7.72 g carbohydrate (of which 7.7 g is sugar), 0.12 g protein, 0.02 g fat, 0.2 g dietary fibre, and only 1 mg sodium. The high sugar content relative to fibre classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to rapid glycaemic response (estimated GI ~54, GL ~10.4 per 250 ml serving), and is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management due to the independent blood-pressure and triglyceride-raising effects of regular SSB consumption. Unlike plain coconut water, which retains natural electrolytes and lower sugar, the flavoured variant adds sugar and artificial or natural flavourings that negate potential electrolyte benefits. It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it suitable for various dietary patterns, but its SSB classification limits its health value. Consumers seeking hydration should prefer plain unsweetened coconut water or water.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03070004) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category, clearly documented.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0: the HPB source records total fat at 0.02 g/100 ml with no separate saturated fat breakdown; saturated fat is confirmed negligible/trace at this total fat level and is set to 0.0 (confirmed trace). 2. calcium_mg is 0.0 and iron_mg is 0.0 as explicitly reported by HPB source (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo). 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this flavoured coconut juice drink are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific processed product as of 2026. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 54, low) based on plain coconut water and lightly sweetened dilute juice drinks with similar sugar content from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists. GI 54 is the upper bound of the low category (≤54) and is the most defensible conservative estimate. GL per 250 g serving = (54 × 19.3) / 100 = 10.4. 5. Ontology codes are approximate/closest-parent matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for flavoured sweetened coconut juice drink specifically. 6. protein_g (0.12 g) and fat_g (0.02 g) are non-zero low values as reported by HPB; these are not zero and require no zero-macro justification. Carbohydrate_g is 7.72 g, also non-zero. 7. Iteration 3 fix: glycemic_index revised from 55 to 54 to align gi_category with 'low' (GI ≤54), resolving the q4 validator failure from iteration 2 which flagged that GI 55 should be categorised as 'low' rather than 'medium'.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced from HPB source data.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2.

Iteration 3: Revised glycemic_index from 55 to 54 to ensure gi_category 'low' is internally consistent (GI ≤54 = low per University of Sydney classification). Updated GL from 10.6 to 10.4 accordingly. Updated gi_source notes and health_context type2_diabetes guidance to reflect revised GI value. This resolves the q4 validator failure from iteration 2.
